Q1: Honestly, my study routine was kind of all over the place. I'd tell myself I was going to study for like 33 hours a week, and I did put in the time, but it wasn't always productive. I'd sit with my laptop open, have the materials in front of me, but my mind would wander. I'm a visual learner so I'd make colorful notes and diagrams, but sometimes I'd spend more time making them pretty than actually understanding the content.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. I tried to have set times, but between my extracurriculars and just life stuff, things got chaotic. When deadlines approached, I'd cram everything in at once. Some weeks I'd be super on top of things, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until the last minute.
Q3: Usually it was because I had an assignment due or I got a notification about something. I wasn't one of those people who logged in just to check things out. If there wasn't something specific pushing me to go on, I probably wouldn't bother. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I wasn't missing a deadline because my anxiety about that was pretty intense.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. I mean, I logged in regularly enough, but it never felt like a natural habit. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do. The only time it felt routine was during the first few weeks when everything was new and I was trying to figure out how the course worked. After that, it became more of a chore.
Q5: The video content was actually pretty helpful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched a lot of videos, like almost three hours worth. They helped me understand concepts better than just reading. The discussion posts were okay too because seeing how other people interpreted things sometimes helped me get unstuck when I was confused.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. They felt overwhelming and I didn't know where to start. If something looked too text-heavy or boring, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and then just... never did. The optional practice problems I mostly skipped because I figured if they weren't graded, they weren't essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to an assignment or if it helped me understand something I was confused about. If I couldn't see an immediate payoff, I'd lose interest fast. Visual stuff like videos or interactive content kept my attention way better than walls of text. Also, if it was something I could finish relatively quickly, I was more likely to do it.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required stuff I'd do even if I was exhausted or stressed because I had to. Optional stuff? I'd convince myself I'd do it 'when I had time' but that time never came. There was always something else that felt more urgent. It's not that I didn't think they were valuable, I just couldn't prioritize them with everything else going on.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos and study from them offline a lot. I also used YouTube for explanations when I didn't understand something from class. Sometimes I'd screenshot important parts and study from my phone when I was out. My notes were mostly handwritten because that helps me remember better, so a lot of my studying happened with just paper and colored pens.
Q10: There were so many times I was studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like I'd print out materials and study at a coffee shop without wifi. Or I'd be going over my handwritten notes for hours. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with classmates in person or over text, working through problems together. None of that would show up anywhere online.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download it or take detailed notes, and then review those notes multiple times without ever going back to the platform. Same with readings - I'd save PDFs to my laptop and highlight them there. It felt more efficient than constantly logging back in, plus I could study anywhere without needing internet.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My anxiety was through the roof and I felt paralyzed. I'd sit down to study and just... freeze. The mindfulness exercises they offered helped a little, but I still felt overwhelmed. When I got back some exam scores and they were way lower than I expected despite all my effort, I kind of mentally checked out for a while.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or tired, I'd log into the LMS but just scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars definitely cut into my study time. And when I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because looking at it made me more anxious. It was easier to pretend the problem didn't exist.
Q14: Yeah, this happened a lot actually. I'd have the videos playing but be on my phone. Or I'd be reading a document but realize I'd gone through three pages without taking in a single word. My mind would be somewhere else entirely - worrying about other things or just zoning out. The engagement looked real on paper but mentally I was checked out.
Q15: I procrastinated on studying for exams a lot because they stressed me out so much. The anxiety made me avoid thinking about them until I absolutely had to. I'd do all my assignments on time - those felt manageable - but actual exam prep? I'd find any excuse to put it off. I think that's why my assignment scores were decent but my exam score was so bad.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift. I'd check the LMS obsessively as deadlines approached, sometimes multiple times a day, making sure I hadn't missed anything. My usage definitely spiked right before things were due. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I could motivate myself to actually complete things.
Q17: Feedback honestly made me anxious. When I got good feedback, I'd feel relieved and maybe a little more motivated. But when it was critical, I'd spiral and avoid looking at the course for a few days. I know that's not productive but I couldn't help it. Sometimes I'd delay checking my grades because I was scared of what I'd see.
Q18: The structure was okay, I guess. Having clear deadlines helped because otherwise I'd never do anything. But sometimes the course felt like it had too many moving parts - six online courses is a lot to juggle. I'd lose track of what was due when across all of them. The weekly modules helped somewhat, but I still felt scattered.
Q19: The videos being available made things easier since that's how I learn best. But the amount of reading was overwhelming and made engagement harder. Also, when discussion posts felt forced or artificial, I'd do the bare minimum. If the course had more interactive visual content and less dense reading, I probably would have engaged more deeply.
Q20: I think that's basically me. I was clicking around, watching videos, posting in discussions, but when it came to exams, I couldn't perform. Maybe it's because surface-level engagement isn't the same as deep learning. You can go through all the motions and still not actually understand the material well enough to apply it under pressure.
Q21: I've seen people like that and honestly, I'm jealous. Some people are just more efficient - they study smart, not just hard. They probably do a lot of work offline or just naturally retain information better. The LMS doesn't capture how well someone actually understands things, just how much time they spend clicking around.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, how long you spent on things. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding. They miss all the offline studying, the mental struggles, the anxiety that makes you freeze up during exams. They see quantity but not quality of engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine mental effort, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and want to understand, not just complete tasks. For me, I was engaged in some ways - I did the work, I showed up - but mentally, I was often somewhere else. True engagement feels different from just compliance.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything. It wouldn't show that I was trying really hard but still failing, or that I was happy on the surface but struggling underneath. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the panic attacks before exams. Numbers can't tell you about the emotional experience of being a student who's drowning while looking like they're swimming.
